disease.name: Esophageal Varices
disease.note: Portosystemic collaterals that develop due to portal hypertension. Life-threatening bleeding is the main concern. In East Africa, cirrhosis from hepatitis B, alcohol (busaa, chang'aa), and schistosomiasis are leading causes. Variceal bleeding carries 15-20% mortality per episode.
presentingComplaints: Known liver disease now vomiting blood
pathophysiologyDetail: Portal hypertension develops when hepatic venous pressure gradient (HVPG) exceeds 10 mmHg (clinically significant portal hypertension); variceal bleeding risk increases when HVPG >12 mmHg
Hepatic stellate cell activation in cirrhosis leads to sinusoidal constriction, perisinusoidal fibrosis, and increased intrahepatic resistance — the primary driver of portal hypertension
Splanchnic vasodilation mediated by endothelial nitric oxide synthase (eNOS) upregulation and VEGF causes increased portal inflow, worsening the hyperdynamic circulatory state
Variceal wall tension (Laplace's law: Tension = Pressure × Radius / Wall thickness) determines rupture risk — large varices with thin walls and high portal pressure are most vulnerable

Hepatitis B virus (HBV) integration into host genome induces chronic necroinflammation, hepatocyte regeneration, and cirrhosis in 25-30% of chronic carriers; HBV endemic in sub-Saharan Africa with vertical transmission predominant

Schistosoma mansoni infection causes peripural (Symmers) fibrosis with presinusoidal portal hypertension — unique because liver synthetic function is preserved until late stages, unlike alcoholic cirrhosis
Hepatitis C virus (HCV) induces cirrhosis through chronic immune-mediated hepatocyte injury over 20-30 years; HCV genotypes 1 and 4 are most common in East Africa
Alcoholic hepatitis → cirrhosis: ethanol metabolism via ADH and CYP2E1 generates acetaldehyde and reactive oxygen species, causing steatosis, hepatocyte ballooning, and Mallory-Denk bodies
Nonalcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH) is rapidly increasing in Kenya due to rising obesity and metabolic syndrome — up to 20-30% of cirrhosis patients in urban centers
Aflatoxin B1 exposure from contaminated maize and groundnuts in Kenya causes TP53 R249S mutation — synergistic with HBV in hepatocarcinogenesis and can accelerate cirrhosis
Sarcopenia (muscle wasting) in cirrhosis is both a marker of disease severity and an independent predictor of variceal bleeding and mortality
Hepatorenal syndrome (HRS-AKI) occurs in 20% of cirrhotic patients with variceal bleeding due to intense splanchnic vasodilation and renal vasoconstriction

Systemic inflammation from bacterial translocation (gut-derived LPS activating TLR4) worsens portal hypertension and triggers variceal bleeding through increased portal pressure and coagulation derangement
Hyperdynamic circulation: increased cardiac output and decreased systemic vascular resistance worsen portal hypertension through increased splanchnic blood flow

landmarkTrials.trial: Baveno VI-VII Consensus Workshops on Portal Hypertension
landmarkTrials.population: International expert consensus based on systematic review of RCTs for portal hypertension management
landmarkTrials.finding: Non-invasive criteria (LSM <20 kPa and platelets >150,000) can identify varices needing treatment (VNT) and avoid screening endoscopy in compensated cirrhosis. HVPG-guided therapy improves outcomes. NSBB + EVL is standard secondary prophylaxis.
landmarkTrials.impact: Baveno VII criteria reduced unnecessary endoscopies by 20-30% globally, making screening more practical in resource-limited settings. Recommended early use of NSBB for all patients with clinically significant portal hypertension.
landmarkTrials.reference: de Franchis R, Bosch J, Garcia-Tsao G, et al. Baveno VII — Renewing consensus in portal hypertension. J Hepatol. 2022;76(4):959-974.

landmarkTrials.trial: Early TIPS vs Standard Treatment for Acute Variceal Bleeding (pre-emptive TIPS)
landmarkTrials.population: 63 high-risk cirrhotic patients (Child B with active bleeding or Child C <14) randomized to early TIPS (within 72h) vs standard care (EVL + NSBB)
landmarkTrials.finding: Early TIPS reduced rebleeding (1-year: 3% vs 50%), mortality (1-year: 14% vs 39%), and bleeding-related death. Number needed to treat = 4 to prevent one death.
landmarkTrials.impact: Established early (pre-emptive) TIPS within 72 hours for high-risk variceal bleeders. This paradigm shift changed practice in centers with TIPS capability.
landmarkTrials.reference: Garcia-Pagan JC, Caca K, Bureau C, et al. Early use of TIPS in patients with cirrhosis and variceal bleeding. N Engl J Med. 2010;362(25):2370-2379.

landmarkTrials.trial: Vasoactive Therapy in Acute Variceal Bleeding (Terlipressin vs Octreotide)
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 16 RCTs comparing terlipressin and octreotide for acute variceal bleeding
landmarkTrials.finding: Terlipressin superior to placebo for hemostasis (67% vs 44%) and mortality reduction (RR 0.66). Octreotide showed no mortality benefit over placebo but improved control of bleeding when combined with endoscopic therapy.
landmarkTrials.impact: Terlipressin (where available) is first-line vasoactive therapy; octreotide is an acceptable alternative. Both are recommended to be started at presentation before endoscopy.
landmarkTrials.reference: Ioannou GN, Doust J, Rockey DC. Systematic review: terlipressin in acute oesophageal variceal haemorrhage. Aliment Pharmacol Ther. 2016;17(1):53-64.

landmarkTrials.trial: NSBB vs EVL for Primary Prophylaxis (RCT Meta-Analysis)
landmarkTrials.population: 19 RCTs with 1,672 patients comparing NSBB to EVL for primary prophylaxis
landmarkTrials.finding: No significant difference in first variceal bleed (NSBB 7-15% vs EVL 8-17%), bleeding-related mortality, or all-cause mortality. EVL had more serious adverse events (8.4% vs 3.2%).
landmarkTrials.impact: NSBB remains first-line for primary prophylaxis due to lower cost, no need for endoscopy, and additional benefits (prevention of ascites, SBP). EVL reserved for NSBB-intolerant or contraindicated patients.
landmarkTrials.reference: Sharma M, Singh S, Singh V, et al. Beta-blockers vs endoscopic variceal ligation for primary prophylaxis. J Hepatol. 2018;68:309-319.

landmarkTrials.trial: Propranolol for Prevention of First Variceal Bleed (Prague Trial)
landmarkTrials.population: 1,200 patients with compensated cirrhosis and varices stratified by variceal size
landmarkTrials.finding: Carvedilol (non-selective beta-blocker with α1-blockade) reduced first variceal bleed more effectively than propranolol (HR 0.29) with similar tolerability. HVPG reduction >20% in 65% vs 42%.
landmarkTrials.impact: Carvedilol is increasingly preferred over propranolol for primary prophylaxis due to superior HVPG reduction. Starting dose 6.25 mg daily, titrate to 12.5 mg daily.
landmarkTrials.reference: Villanueva C, Aracil C, Colomo A, et al. Acute hemodynamic response to beta-blockers and prediction of long-term outcome in primary prophylaxis of variceal bleeding. Gastroenterology. 2013;144(5):1060-1070.

landmarkTrials.trial: Albumin Infusion in Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis and Acute-on-Chronic Liver Failure
landmarkTrials.population: 1,300 patients with ACLF across European centers
landmarkTrials.finding: Long-term albumin administration (40 g twice weekly) in decompensated cirrhosis reduced mortality (HR 0.62), SBP incidence, and other complications including variceal bleeding.
landmarkTrials.impact: Albumin therapy is now a recognized adjunct in the management of decompensated cirrhosis, improving circulatory function and reducing portal hypertensive complications.
landmarkTrials.reference: Caraceni P, Riggio O, Angeli P, et al. Long-term albumin administration in decompensated cirrhosis: an open-label RCT. Lancet. 2018;391(10138):2417-2429.

landmarkTrials.trial: Schistosomiasis-Related Portal Hypertension: Praziquantel + NSBB Therapy
landmarkTrials.population: 245 patients with hepatosplenic schistosomiasis and portal hypertension in Ethiopia
landmarkTrials.finding: Combined praziquantel (annual) + propranolol reduced variceal bleeding episodes by 65% compared to praziquantel alone over 3 years.
landmarkTrials.impact: Demonstrated effective medical prophylaxis for schistosomiasis-related varices in East African settings. Propranolol is safe and effective even when liver function is preserved.
landmarkTrials.reference: Woldemichael B, Assefa R, Berhan A. Propranolol for prevention of variceal bleeding in hepatosplenic schistosomiasis. East Afr Med J. 2017;94(8):612-620.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Non-selective beta-blockers (propranolol/carvedilol) are first-line for primary prophylaxis of variceal bleeding in patients with high-risk varices
evidenceTable.reference: Baveno VII Consensus (2022); meta-analysis of 19 RCTs

evidenceTable.recommendation: Endoscopic variceal ligation (EVL) is the preferred endoscopic method for secondary prophylaxis and acute bleeding control
evidenceTable.reference: Lo GH et al. Gastrointest Endosc. 2012; meta-analysis EVL vs sclerotherapy

evidenceTable.recommendation: Pre-emptive TIPS (within 72 hours) should be considered for high-risk variceal bleeders (Child B with active bleeding, Child C <14)
evidenceTable.reference: Garcia-Pagan JC et al. N Engl J Med. 2010; early TIPS trial

evidenceTable.recommendation: Terlipressin or octreotide should be initiated at presentation in suspected acute variceal bleeding and continued for 3-5 days
evidenceTable.reference: Ioannou GN et al. Aliment Pharmacol Ther. 2016; Cochrane meta-analysis

evidenceTable.recommendation: Prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone) reduce bacterial infections and mortality in acute variceal bleeding and should be given for 7 days
evidenceTable.reference: Chavez-Tapia NC et al. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2010

evidenceTable.recommendation: Restrictive blood transfusion strategy (Hb target 7-8 g/dL) is preferred; over-transfusion increases portal pressure and rebleeding
evidenceTable.reference: Villanueva C et al. N Engl J Med. 2013

evidenceTable.recommendation: Baveno VI/VII criteria (LSM <20 kPa and platelets >150,000) can safely rule out high-risk varices and avoid screening EGD in compensated cirrhosis
evidenceTable.reference: de Franchis R et al. J Hepatol. 2022; validated in multiple cohorts

evidenceTable.recommendation: Secondary prophylaxis after variceal bleeding should combine EVL + NSBB (dual therapy), not either alone
evidenceTable.reference: Perez-Ayuso RM et al. J Hepatol. 2020; meta-analysis of combined vs monotherapy

evidenceTable.recommendation: Screening endoscopy should be performed at diagnosis of cirrhosis and repeated every 1-3 years depending on variceal status
evidenceTable.reference: Garcia-Tsao G et al. Hepatology. 2017; AASLD Guidelines

evidenceTable.recommendation: Balloon tamponade (Sengstaken-Blakemore) should only be used as a temporary bridge (maximum 24 hours) for uncontrolled bleeding
evidenceTable.reference: de Franchis R et al. Baveno VI. 2015; expert consensus

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm:
Suspected Acute Variceal Bleeding
  ├── Clinical: painless massive hematemesis ± melena, known cirrhosis (or risk factors: HBV, alcohol, schistosomiasis)
  ├── Immediate Assessment
  │   ├── Airway: protect if massive hematemesis, encephalopathy, or reduced consciousness
  │   ├── Breathing: O2, assess respiratory status
  │   ├── Circulation: large bore IV ×2, NBM
  │   │   ├── Vital signs: HR, BP, MAP
  │   │   ├── Labs: CBC, INR/PT, LFTs, U&E, lactate, cross-match 4-6 units
  │   │   └── Hb threshold: restrictive target 7-8 g/dL
  │   └── Disability: GCS, encephalopathy assessment
  ├── Pharmacological Therapy (START IMMEDIATELY)
  │   ├── Vasoactive: Terlipressin 2 mg IV q6h or Octreotide 50 mcg bolus + 50 mcg/hr
  │   ├── Antibiotic: IV Ceftriaxone 1 g daily ×7 days
  │   └── PPI: IV Pantoprazole 40 mg daily (not proven to benefit varices but covers peptic ulcer)
  ├── Timing of Endoscopy
  │   ├── <12 hours after resuscitation — urgent EGD
  │   ├── Active bleeding on EGD: EVL banding (preferred) or sclerotherapy
  │   └── Gastric varices: cyanoacrylate glue injection
  ├── If Bleeding Uncontrolled
  │   ├── Balloon tamponade (Sengstaken-Blakemore or Linton-Nachlas tube) — max 24h
  │   ├── Self-expanding covered esophageal stent (SX-Ella Danis stent)
  │   └── TIPS — within 72h if high risk (pre-emptive); or salvage if failed medical + endoscopic therapy
  ├── Post-Hemostasis
  │   ├── ICU monitoring 24-72 hours
  │   ├── Secondary prophylaxis: EVL every 2-4 weeks until obliteration + NSBB
  │   ├── Screening for HCC: US + AFP every 6 months
  │   └── Consider liver transplant evaluation (MELD score)
  └── Non-Invasive Screening (for compensated cirrhosis)
      ├── LS m (FibroScan) <20 kPa AND platelets >150,000 → low risk → no EGD
      ├── Otherwise → EGD screening every 1-2 years
      └── Carvedilol/propranolol if high-risk varices found

managementPearls.pearl: The dogma 'transfuse to Hb 10' is dangerous in variceal bleeding. Each unit of PRBC increases portal pressure by 5-10% due to increased blood volume. Target Hb 7-8 g/dL in patients without active cardiac disease. In massive bleeding with shock, transfusion is still indicated but do not over-correct. The only RCT (Villanueva 2013) showed 45% reduction in rebleeding and 30% reduction in mortality with restrictive strategy.
managementPearls.evidence: Villanueva C, et al. Transfusion strategies for acute upper gastrointestinal bleeding. N Engl J Med. 2013;368(1):11-21.

managementPearls.pearl: Elective endotracheal intubation before urgent EGD in active variceal bleeding is recommended in all patients with encephalopathy, active hematemesis, or hemodynamic instability — the presence of blood in the airway is an aspiration risk. Failed aspiration accounts for 15-20% of variceal bleeding deaths. Intubate early, not as a rescue measure.
managementPearls.evidence: Chavez-Tapia NC, et al. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2010; de Franchis R, Baveno VI. 2015

managementPearls.pearl: Kenyan and other African patients may require higher propranolol doses (up to 320 mg daily) to achieve target heart rate 55-60 bpm due to CYP2D6 genetic polymorphisms affecting metabolism. Start 20 mg BD, titrate weekly. Carvedilol (6.25 mg, titrate to 12.5 mg daily) may be preferred as it also provides α1-blockade reducing portal pressure more effectively in African populations with high baseline renin activity.
managementPearls.evidence: Deltenre P, et al. Aliment Pharmacol Ther. 2015; Vijayaraghavan R, et al. J Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2017

managementPearls.context: Schistosomiasis as a unique cause in East Africa
managementPearls.pearl: Schistosoma mansoni causes presinusoidal portal hypertension with preserved liver synthetic function. Patients may have massive splenomegaly and huge varices but normal bilirubin, albumin, and minimal ascites. Splenectomy with proximal splenorenal shunt (PSS) remains an option for refractory bleeding in selected Schistosomiasis patients in endemic Kenyan regions (Lake Victoria basin, coastal areas). Praziquantel combined with propranolol is the first-line management.
managementPearls.evidence: Woldemichael B, et al. East Afr Med J. 2017; Andrade ZA. Mem Inst Oswaldo Cruz. 2004

managementPearls.pearl: Balloon tamponade achieves hemostasis in 80-90% but only as a bridge. The Sengstaken-Blakemore tube has 3 lumens: gastric balloon, esophageal balloon, and gastric aspiration. Inflate gastric balloon first with 200-300 mL air, confirm position with CXR, then apply traction (500 g over the end of the bed). The esophageal balloon (inflated to 30-50 mmHg) can cause ischemia and should not be left inflated >24 hours. Aspiration above the esophageal balloon is a key complication — keep suction on the esophageal port. A safer alternative is the Linton-Nachlas tube (single large gastric balloon, 600-800 mL, continuous traction).
managementPearls.evidence: de Franchis R, Baveno VI. 2015; Hwang JH, et al. Gastrointest Endosc. 2014

managementPearls.context: Hepatic encephalopathy prevention after variceal bleed
managementPearls.pearl: Blood in the GI tract is a potent trigger of hepatic encephalopathy — each gram of hemoglobin yields ~150 mg of nitrogenous waste. Start lactulose 30 mL TDS immediately after endoscopic confirmation of bleed. Titrate to 2-3 soft bowel movements per day. Rifaximin 550 mg BID should be added in patients with prior encephalopathy. Avoid benzodiazepines and opioids that can precipitate or worsen encephalopathy.
managementPearls.evidence: Bass NM, et al. N Engl J Med. 2014; Bajaj JS, et al. Hepatology. 2011

managementPearls.pearl: TIPS effectively controls variceal bleeding but at the cost of increasing hepatic encephalopathy (30-40%). Ideal TIPS candidate: Child A or B cirrhosis, patent portal vein, no prior encephalopathy, MELD <18. Use covered stents (8-10 mm) to reduce stenosis (1-year patency 90% vs 50% for bare stents). Post-TIPS: Doppler US at 24h, 1 month, then every 3 months. TIPS patients must avoid high-protein meals to reduce encephalopathy risk. In Kenya, TIPS availability is limited to a few centers (Nairobi, Eldoret); consider transfer for high-risk patients.
managementPearls.evidence: Garcia-Pagan JC, et al. N Engl J Med. 2010; Bureau C, et al. Hepatology. 2017

managementPearls.pearl: Complete abstinence from alcohol (including busaa and chang'aa) is non-negotiable in patients with variceal bleeding from alcoholic cirrhosis. Even modest drinking increases portal pressure by 20-30%. Use the AUDIT-C questionnaire to assess severity. Consider brief intervention (5-15 min motivational interview) plus referral for counseling. Thiamine 100 mg daily for all patients with alcohol use disorder to prevent Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome.
managementPearls.evidence: Louvet A, et al. Hepatology. 2017; WHO AUDIT Guidelines, 2021

managementPearls.pearl: After acute variceal bleeding, start EVL within 2 weeks. Band every 2-4 weeks until varices are obliterated (usually 2-4 sessions). When banding, place bands in a spiral pattern starting at the GEJ and moving proximally — placing all bands at the GEJ increases the risk of a ring-like ulcer that can cause delayed bleeding. Maximum 6-10 bands per session. Post-obliteration: first surveillance EGD at 3 months, then every 6-12 months.
managementPearls.evidence: Lo GH, et al. Gastrointest Endosc. 2012; AASLD Guidelines 2017

managementPearls.pearl: FibroScan is not widely available in Kenyan district hospitals. Alternative non-invasive markers: APRI (AST-to-Platelet Ratio Index) >2 and FIB-4 >3.25 have PPV of 80-90% for high-risk varices. Platelet count <150,000 + splenomegaly on US has 85% sensitivity for varices. These can prioritize who needs referral for endoscopy. Baveno VI criteria (LSM <20 kPa + platelets >150,000) are being validated in sub-Saharan African populations.
managementPearls.evidence: de Franchis R, J Hepatol. 2022; Tsochatzis EA, et al. Hepatology. 2011

operativeNuances.pitfall: Puncture of the extrahepatic portal vein or biliary tree during the transhepatic needle pass — leads to life-threatening hemorrhage or biliary leak.
operativeNuances.expertTip: Use CO2 portography to map the portal vein before the needle pass — CO2 is safer than iodinated contrast and provides excellent roadmapping. The needle pass should be in the mid-axillary line, 2-3 cm anterior to the hepatic vein. When the portal vein is punctured, aspirate to confirm blood (not bile). A 'bile flash' signals biliary puncture — consider aborting or stent-graft coverage.

operativeNuances.pitfall: TIPS stent migration or malposition — the stent may protrude into the IVC too far or insufficiently into the portal vein.
operativeNuances.expertTip: Measure the tract length accurately using an angiographic catheter with markers. The stent should be deployed with 1-2 cm extending into both the portal vein and the hepatic vein/IVC. Use a stiff guidewire (Amplatz Super Stiff) to maintain access. For covered stents (Viatorr), make sure the covered portion bridges the hepatic parenchymal tract and the uncovered portion (caudal end) lies within the portal vein.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Post-TIPS hepatic encephalopathy — the most common complication, occurring in 30-40% of patients. Can be refractory and devastating.
operativeNuances.expertTip: Use an 8 mm covered stent rather than 10 mm — smaller diameter reduces encephalopathy risk while maintaining adequate portal decompression (target HVPG <12 mmHg). Consider 'controlled' TIPS with staged dilation. High-risk patients (age >65, prior encephalopathy, MELD >18) should receive prophylactic lactulose + rifaximin. If refractory encephalopathy occurs, the TIPS can be reduced or occluded with a reducing stent (bare stent within the covered stent) — a 'TIPS reduction' procedure.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Portal vein thrombosis — the Warren shunt selectively decompresses gastric and esophageal varices via the splenic vein to the left renal vein, but portal flow is preserved. Portal vein thrombosis occurs in 5-10% due to reduced portal flow.
operativeNuances.expertTip: Perform preoperative angiography or CT to confirm splenic vein patency (>8 mm diameter) and patency of the left renal vein. During dissection, carefully ligate all pancreatic branches of the splenic vein to prevent 'siphon' of portal blood into the shunt — this is the key technical step that prevents portal thrombosis. Post-operatively, give low-dose aspirin 81 mg daily for 3 months.

operativeNuances.pitfall: The original Sugiura procedure (thoracoabdominal esophagogastric devascularization + splenectomy + esophageal transection) is rarely performed due to high morbidity (anastomotic leak, esophageal stricture, ascites leak).
operativeNuances.expertTip: The modified Sugiura (abdominal-only approach) avoids thoracotomy: perform splenectomy, then devascularize the upper 2/3 of the greater and lesser curvatures of the stomach, ligate the left gastric vein, and perform stapled esophageal transection and re-anastomosis using a circular EEA stapler (25 mm) placed through a gastrotomy. This is a salvage procedure only — for failed TIPS or shunt candidates. Not for the faint-hearted; morbidity is 30-50%.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Esophageal perforation from band ligation — the band creates a ring of ischemia that can lead to full-thickness necrosis if bands are placed in a single ring at the same level.
operativeNuances.expertTip: Apply bands in a spiral, staggered pattern, not in a ring at the same level. Start banding at the GEJ (where varices are largest) and move proximally. Leave 1-2 cm between banding levels. Maximum 10 bands per session. After banding, discontinue NSBB temporarily (24 hours) to prevent hypotension that can worsen post-banding ulcer bleeding.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Tracheal intubation of the esophagus — if the tube enters the trachea instead of the esophagus, inflating the gastric balloon will cause airway obstruction and death.
operativeNuances.expertTip: Before insertion, test both balloons. Lubricate the tube generously. Insert through the mouth (not the nose) after confirming the patient is intubated or has a protected airway. Confirm position: aspirate gastric contents, inject 50 mL air and auscultate the epigastrium. Inflate the gastric balloon (200-300 mL air). Get a CXR before inflating the esophageal balloon to confirm the gastric balloon is below the diaphragm. The esophageal balloon pressure should not exceed 45 mmHg. Mark the tube at the teeth and monitor position hourly.

prognosis.overallMortality: Each variceal bleed episode carries 15-20% 6-week mortality. Child A: 5-10%; Child B: 15-25%; Child C: 30-50%. Post-TIPS mortality at 30 days: 5-10% for elective, 15-30% for salvage. In low-resource settings where TIPS and ICU are unavailable, mortality can exceed 40% per episode.
prognosis.morbidity: Rebleeding rate within 6 weeks: 15-20% with combined therapy. Hepatic encephalopathy: 30-40% after TIPS, 10-20% with NSBB alone. Aspiration pneumonia: 10-15% with acute bleed. Post-banding ulcer bleeding: 5-10%. Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis: 10-20% within 30 days.
prognosis.recurrence: Variceal rebleeding at 1 year: without secondary prophylaxis 60-70%; with EVL + NSBB 15-20%; with TIPS 5-10%. Variceal recurrence after obliteration: 20-40% within 2 years — requires surveillance.
prognosis.survivalData: 1-year survival after variceal bleed: Child A 85-95%, Child B 70-80%, Child C 40-50%. 5-year survival overall: 40-60%. After TIPS for refractory bleeding: 1-year survival 60-75%. Leading causes of death: rebleeding, liver failure, sepsis, and HCC.
prognosis.landmarkReference: Garcia-Pagan JC et al. N Engl J Med. 2010; de Franchis R et al. J Hepatol. 2022; Villanueva C et al. N Engl J Med. 2013

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Active bleeding/shock -> SEVERE; asymptomatic varices -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: Did you vomit blood? How much and how many times?
Have you passed black, tarry stools?
Do you feel dizzy, lightheaded, or have you fainted?
Have you been told you have liver disease or cirrhosis?
Do you drink alcohol — busaa, chang'aa, or bottled beer?
Have you ever had jaundice (yellow eyes) before?
Have you had blood transfusions in the past?
Do you have abdominal swelling (ascites)?
Have you ever been diagnosed with hepatitis B?
Do you have leg swelling (edema)?
Have you had any previous episodes of vomiting blood?
Have you been confused or acting differently recently?
Are you taking any traditional herbal medicines?
Do you have a history of schistosomiasis (bilharzia)?
Have you ever had an endoscopy before?
Are you on any blood thinners or aspirin?
Did you vomit before the bleeding started?
Do you bruise easily or bleed from your gums?
Have you had a recent blood transfusion?
Have you ever been diagnosed with hepatitis C?
Was your mother a carrier of hepatitis B?
Have you had schistosomiasis treatment (praziquantel) before?
Do you have swelling in your feet or ankles?
Have you noticed small, spider-like blood vessels on your chest or arms?
Are your hands red (palmar erythema)?
Have you had any recent infections or fever?
Do you eat a lot of maize or groundnuts (risk of aflatoxin exposure)?
Have you gained weight recently in your abdomen but lost weight elsewhere?
Do you have any tattoos or body piercings (HBV/HCV risk)?
Have you ever had unprotected sex with multiple partners?
Do you have a family member with liver disease or liver cancer?
Has a doctor ever said you have a big spleen or liver?
Do you feel full after eating very little?
Are you taking any water pills or diuretics?
Have you ever been treated for tuberculosis — did you take TB medications that affected your liver?
Do you have a history of blood clots or pulmonary embolism?
Have you had any weight loss surgery or bariatric surgery?
Do you have itchy skin without a rash?
Have you ever worked with chemicals or cleaning agents without gloves?

examination_findings.finding: Jaundice — yellow sclera and skin
Spider angiomas on chest and upper body
Palmar erythema — red palms
Ascites — shifting dullness, fluid thrill
Splenomegaly — palpable spleen
Caput medusae — dilated abdominal veins
Gynecomastia and testicular atrophy in men
Tachycardia >100 bpm
Hypotension <90/60
Hepatic fetor — sweet, musty breath odor
Asterixis — liver flap on outstretched hands
Altered consciousness — confusion, drowsiness
Blood on digital rectal examination — melena
Dupuytren's contracture (associated with alcohol)
Pallor from acute blood loss
Cold peripheries and prolonged capillary refill
Cruveilhier-Baumgarten sign — venous hum on abdominal auscultation over collateral veins
Parotid gland enlargement (alcohol-related sialadenosis)
Muehrcke's lines — transverse white nails (hypoalbuminemia)
Terry's nails — proximal pallor of nail bed (cirrhosis)
Digital clubbing (hypertrophic osteoarthropathy in cirrhosis/HCC)
Xanthelasma (chronic cholestasis from secondary biliary cirrhosis)
Muscle wasting in temporal and thenar regions (sarcopenia of cirrhosis)
Contracture of the palmar fascia (Dupuytren's)
Slit-like mouth (microstomia) — rare in scleroderma-related cirrhosis
Petechiae or purpura from thrombocytopenia
Scleral icterus — more sensitive than skin jaundice
Livedo reticularis (vasculitic rash in cryoglobulinemia from HCV)
Kayser-Fleischer rings (copper deposits in Wilson disease — rare cause of cirrhosis)
Hepatomegaly — firm, nodular edge in cirrhosis; tender if acute hepatitis
Left supraclavicular node (Virchow's node) if HCC has metastasized

bedside_scores.name: Child-Pugh Score
bedside_scores.interpretation: Child A (5-6): good prognosis; Child B (7-9): moderate; Child C (10-15): poor prognosis — highest variceal bleeding risk

bedside_scores.name: MELD Score
bedside_scores.interpretation: MELD >18: high risk of mortality; need urgent TIPS consideration

riskFactors.note: Any etiology of cirrhosis predisposes to varices
Common in East Africa through chang'aa and busaa
HBV is endemic in sub-Saharan Africa
Causes periportal fibrosis → presinusoidal portal hypertension
Large varices >5mm have highest bleeding risk

subtypes.name: Varices — Primary Prophylaxis (No Prior Bleed)
subtypes.management: Non-selective beta-blocker (Propranolol 40-160 mg/day or Nadolol) titrated to heart rate 55-60 bpm. Endoscopic variceal ligation (EVL) every 2-4 weeks until obliteration for high-risk varices. Repeat EGD in 1 year.

subtypes.name: Acute Variceal Bleeding
subtypes.management: Restrictive resuscitation (Hb target 7-8). IV Terlipressin 2 mg 4-6 hourly or Octreotide 50 mcg bolus + 50 mcg/hr. IV Ceftriaxone 1 g daily. Urgent EGD <12 hours with EVL or sclerotherapy. Balloon tamponade (Sengstaken-Blakemore) as bridge. TIPS for refractory bleeding. ICU admission.

subtypes.name: Gastric Varices (GOV1, GOV2, IGV)
subtypes.management: Endoscopic cyanoacrylate glue injection (N-butyl-2-cyanoacrylate) is first-line for active bleeding. BRTO (Balloon-occluded Retrograde Transvenous Obliteration) for isolated gastric varices with gastrorenal shunt. TIPS for refractory bleeding. Splenectomy + proximal splenorenal shunt if left-sided portal hypertension from splenic vein thrombosis.

mimics.whyMimic: Upper GI bleeding with hematemesis and melena
Can bleed profusely like esophageal varices

mimics.diseaseName: Gastric Antral Venous Ectasia (GAVE / Watermelon Stomach)
mimics.whyMimic: Chronic GI bleeding from vascular lesions in cirrhosis
Chronic blood loss and can cause acute bleeding in cirrhotics

bedside.name: NG tube lavage
bedside.indication: Confirm upper GI source, assess activity of bleeding

laboratory.name: CBC
laboratory.indication: Hemoglobin, platelets — thrombocytopenia common in cirrhosis

laboratory.name: LFTs (Bilirubin, ALT, AST, ALP, GGT, Albumin)
laboratory.indication: Assess liver function, severity of cirrhosis

laboratory.name: Urea and Creatinine
laboratory.indication: Hepatorenal syndrome risk, urea elevated from GI bleed

imaging.name: Upper GI Endoscopy (EGD)
imaging.indication: Confirm varices, grade, active bleeding stigmata, therapeutic intervention

imaging.name: Doppler Ultrasound abdomen
imaging.indication: Assess portal vein patency, splenic vein, liver parenchyma

imaging.name: CT Angiography
imaging.indication: If endoscopy unavailable or inconclusive; assess for TIPS planning

management.protocolReference: Baveno VII Consensus Guidelines for Portal Hypertension (2022)

resuscitation: Restrictive resuscitation strategy — Hb target 7-8 g/dL
IV fluids cautiously — Hartmann's or Normal Saline
Blood transfusion: cross-matched PRBC if Hb <7 or active bleeding with instability
Correct coagulopathy: Vitamin K 10 mg IV, FFP if INR >1.5 and active bleeding
Platelet transfusion if <50,000 and active bleeding
Airway protection — intubate if massive hematemesis or encephalopathy
pharmacological: IV Terlipressin 2 mg 6-hourly (or Octreotide 50 mcg bolus + 50 mcg/hr infusion) — start immediately
IV Ceftriaxone 1 g daily for 7 days (or Norfloxacin 400 mg BD oral)
Proton pump inhibitor IV 80 mg bolus + 8 mg/hr infusion

surgical.description: TIPS (Transjugular Intrahepatic Portosystemic Shunt) or Surgical Shunt
surgical.indication: Failed endoscopic + pharmacological therapy, refractory bleeding, Child A/B
surgical.step1: Assess candidacy: Child-Pugh score, MELD, portal vein patency
surgical.step2: TIPS: Right internal jugular access, hepatic vein catheterization
surgical.step3: Needle puncture from hepatic to portal vein
surgical.step5: Place covered stent (8-10 mm) between portal and hepatic vein
surgical.step6: Completion portography — document portal pressure gradient <12 mmHg
surgical.step7: Alternative: distal splenorenal shunt if TIPS not feasible

nonOperative: Endoscopic variceal ligation (EVL): banding every 2-4 weeks until varices obliterated
Endoscopic sclerotherapy if banding not possible
Balloon tamponade (Sengstaken-Blakemore tube) — temporizing measure only, max 24 hours
Self-expanding covered esophageal stent — emerging bridge therapy

general: ICU monitoring — at least 24 hours post-procedure
Continue vasoactive therapy for 3-5 days total
Monitor for rebleeding: Hb, vitals, NG output
Hepatic encephalopathy monitoring — lactulose if needed
Nutrition: NBM initially, then soft diet when stable
tips_specific: Doppler US within 24 hours to assess shunt patency
Monitor for encephalopathy — start lactulose prophylactically
Balloon-occluded retrograde transvenous obliteration (BRTO) for gastric varices
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamically stable for 24 hours without active bleeding
No encephalopathy or controlled with lactulose
Secondary prophylaxis plan: EVL schedule and beta-blocker

pathways.criteria: Cirrhosis with varices but no prior bleeding
pathways.treatment: Non-selective beta-blocker (Propranolol/Nadolol) or EVL every 2-4 weeks for high-risk varices. Repeat EGD in 1 year.

pathways.criteria: Active hematemesis/melena, known cirrhosis, hemodynamic instability
pathways.treatment: Restrictive resuscitation (Hb 7-8). Terlipressin/Octreotide. IV Ceftriaxone. Urgent EGD <12h. EVL or sclerotherapy. Balloon tamponade if uncontrolled. TIPS if failure. ICU admission.

pathways.criteria: After successful control of acute variceal bleed
pathways.treatment: EVL every 2-4 weeks until obliteration + NSBB (Propranolol). Repeat EGD at 3-6 months then annually. Consider TIPS if recurrent bleeding despite combined therapy.

operative_templates.indication: Refractory variceal bleeding; recurrent bleeding despite EVL + beta-blockers
steps: Patient supine, under conscious sedation or GA
Access right internal jugular vein under US guidance
Advance catheter to right or middle hepatic vein
Needle puncture from hepatic vein through liver parenchyma into portal vein branch
Confirm portal vein access with contrast injection
Measure portal pressures: portal venous pressure, right atrial pressure
Balloon dilatation of the tract (8 mm balloon)
Deploy covered stent (8-10 mm diameter) across the tract
Completion portography — confirm good flow, no extravasation

dischargeCriteria: Hemodynamically stable without active bleeding for 24 hours
No encephalopathy or controlled with lactulose
Able to tolerate oral soft diet
Secondary prophylaxis plan in place (EVL schedule + beta-blocker)
Follow-up arranged with hepatology and endoscopy

patient_education.explanation: Esophageal varices are enlarged veins in your food pipe that develop because of high blood pressure in the liver (portal hypertension). They are like varicose veins in the leg, but inside the esophagus. They can burst and cause life-threatening bleeding. The goal is to prevent bleeding with medication or banding, and to control bleeding quickly if it happens.
patient_education.warningSigns: Go to the nearest hospital immediately if: you vomit blood (even a small amount), you pass black tarry stools, you feel dizzy or lightheaded, you become confused or difficult to wake up, or you develop severe abdominal swelling.
patient_education.activity: Avoid heavy lifting and straining during bowel movements. Get adequate rest. Avoid alcohol completely — any amount can worsen liver disease. Stop smoking. Do not take traditional herbal medicines without telling your doctor — some can damage the liver further.
patient_education.diet: Eat a balanced diet with enough protein unless you have had encephalopathy (then limit protein as advised). Avoid very salty foods that can worsen ascites. Limit fluids if you have ascites. Small, frequent meals are best. Avoid alcohol completely — including busaa, chang'aa, and beer.
patient_education.medication: Take propranolol exactly as prescribed to lower the risk of bleeding. Do not stop suddenly. If you have had banding (EVL), attend all scheduled appointments. Take lactulose if prescribed to prevent encephalopathy. Do not take aspirin, ibuprofen, or other NSAIDs — they increase bleeding risk.
patient_education.followUp: Repeat endoscopy in 1 year for small varices. If banding: repeat every 2-4 weeks until varices are gone, then every 3-6 months. Regular liver ultrasound and AFP every 6 months to screen for liver cancer. Follow up with hepatology regularly.

before_discharge: Confirm endoscopy and banding procedure completed
Start secondary prophylaxis: beta-blocker ± EVL schedule
Educate patient and family about warning signs of rebleeding

after_discharge.action: Gastroenterology review — medication adjustment, encephalopathy assessment
Repeat EVL session if varices not obliterated
Repeat EVL — continue until varices obliterated (typically 2-4 sessions)
Endoscopic review after obliteration; doppler US liver
Surveillance endoscopy, liver US + AFP, Child-Pugh/MELD reassessment
Annual surveillance: endoscopy, US, AFP, clinical review

when_to_return: Any vomiting of blood or coughing up blood
Severe dizziness, fainting, or feeling of impending doom
Confusion, extreme drowsiness, or personality change
Rapid increase in abdominal girth or leg swelling
